column48:
My vision is to direct and film short films and work with outlets like PBS Nature, the BBC Natural History Unit, National Geographic, The New York Times, etc, or emerging platforms where I can pitch, film, and produce stories about the intersection of people and the ocean. 

I also want to build a career as a freelance underwater cinematographer and photographer, with a particular focus on elevating stories from Mexico and Latin America and supporting Spanish-speaking filmmakers trying to find their footing in this space.

At the heart of it all is the ocean, the animals that inhabit it, and the relationship between people and the sea. My goal is to produce visually incredible work that communicates the urgent need for ocean conservation while showing how we are all connected to it. The ocean gave me my sense of purpose as a storyteller and I think this program would help me build the skills to continue to pursue it.
column49:
As a filmmaker and journalist, I’ve learned that storytelling is one of the most powerful tools we have to connect people to places, ideas, and information. 

My journey as a journalist began in the ocean. I remember being underwater for the first time when all of a sudden it all clicked for me. In that moment, I realized the immense privilege of experiencing a world so few ever get to see and how this incredible world needed to be seen, appreciated and protected. And from acknowledging that privilege, I’ve brought my passion to shine a light on the ocean and the people who depend on it. 

To so many, the ocean is a distant, almost otherworldly place, a place that hides its most fascinating stories beneath. The ocean is our nourishment, protection, livelihood, and the air we breathe. 

After graduating from my bachelors degree, I pursued my passion for underwater photography in South Africa where I learned how to photograph marine life. Capturing marine life taught me not only about its beauty but also its vulnerability. Later, as a graduate student at UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism, I focused my work on ocean conservation, covering everything from sustainable fishing practices in the Bay Area to following a group of women in the Philippines working to protect a marine protected area on the island of Siquijor. Since then, I’ve reported and produced short digital films for different outlets but never been able to film them underwater. 

Last year, I was able to buy my underwater housing but I feel I still need better skills, learn the business side of things, how to really make a niche for myself and the stories I produce. I feel like its a world where its hard to access and the skills required are extremely important, so I would value the time and space to practice those skills, in an out of the water with mentors in the industry.

column48: As a behavioral ecologist studying cetaceans, I want to futher implement filmmaking and storytelling as a tool to showcase  the complex and the fascinating social and foraging behavior of marine mammals. By highlighting the parallels between humans and these charismatic animals, I also hope to reconnect audiences with them and promote human–wildlife coexistence.
column49:
It offers a rare glimpse into a hidden world, creating a powerful and emotional connection between marine life and us terrestrial humans. Through my own work, I have experienced how underwater footage can reveal new perspectives and insights, particularly from a scientific point of view. 

The first BTM video I watched was the sardine run. The rapid succession of dynamic shots capturing the interdependent communities surrounding the bait ball perfectly conveyed what it truly feels like to witness such an event. It transported me into that moment underwater. The professionalism, thoughtful shot selection, and the genuine joy and passion of the people involved inspired me and further motivated me to apply to the BTM Academy

column48:
Hi, my name is Raphh. I am from an island called Maamigili in the Maldives, located in South Ari Atoll, which is known as one of the homes of whale sharks.
My passion for the ocean, underwater wildlife, and scuba diving began through my uncle, who has been a scuba instructor for many years. Watching him and hearing his stories made me curious about the underwater world and inspired me to explore it myself.
I still remember a moment from my childhood that changed my life. My mom was having a hard time feeding me, and my grandpa turned on Nat Geo TV. The moment I saw the underwater scenery on the screen, I was completely amazed. Even as a child, I remember thinking that the person who captured those shots must be a genius. From that moment, I wanted to create those kinds of images and videos and share them with the world, so people could see the beauty of the underwater environment, fall in love with it, and help protect these fragile ecosystems.
Later, I saw someone using underwater camera equipment with a scuba tank that allowed him to stay underwater for hours. Seeing that made me even more fascinated by scuba diving and underwater videography.
The person who truly inspired me to start diving was my uncle and instructor naseem. When I was very young, he used to bring beautiful photographs of sea fans and hang them on the walls as decorations. I would stare at those photographs for hours. From that moment on, I knew that I wanted to try scuba diving.
I got the opportunity to try my first Discover Scuba Diving experience when I was around 13 years old at a nearby resort, which is currently called Villa Haven, under Dive Oceanus. The day I took my first breath underwater truly changed my life.
As soon as I returned to my island, I met my uncle and told him that I didn’t even want to continue studying—I wanted to start diving immediately. He smiled and told me, “Finish your school first, and then come to my dive center. I will make it happen.” At that time, he was running his own dive center on our island.
I remember asking my dad to buy me an underwater camera because I saw the GoPro coming out, and I was already passionate about underwater videography. Since it was too expensive for us at the time, my dad bought me a small action camera called Apeman. Even though it was simple, it meant the world to me.
The day I finished school, I called my uncle and told him that I was ready. He told me to join the dive center the next day. I was around 15–16 years old when I joined Shamar Guesthouse and Dive.
At the dive center, I worked in many roles: receptionist, room boy, waiter, compressor boy, and almost every job related to running a guesthouse and dive center—except guiding certified divers. Eventually, I was allowed to start my Open Water course, which deepened my connection with the ocean.
My instructor at the time would not allow me to dive unless I worked very hard. If I wanted to dive, I had to earn it through hard work. During this time, I also saw many divers with large professional camera setups, which pushed me even more toward underwater filmmaking.
Unfortunately, I had to take a break because of back problems. The doctor advised me to avoid lifting heavy equipment so that my back could recover properly. At the same time, like many teenagers, I had many ambitions in my mind, but half of my heart was always dedicated to my dream of becoming an underwater wildlife filmmaker.
During that period, I worked in many different jobs: as an artist, in local shops, as a delivery boy, an IT technician, and a graphic designer. But even then, I continued diving every weekend—sometimes 4 to 5 dives per week.
Eventually, I decided to pursue my Divemaster certification. To be honest, I did not start diving with the goal of becoming like most instructors. My goal was to invest in my gear, travel, learn, and document the underwater world.
While working at my island’s airport as a graphic designer and IT technician, I saved money to buy my first proper gear: a GoPro Hero 10, lights, dome ports, and other accessories. From there, I began to dive deeper into the world of underwater videography, constantly experimenting and learning.
I continued diving as an Advanced Nitrox Diver, focusing on improving my filming skills while minding my own work.
A few years later, after completing my Divemaster, I moved away from my home island to pursue my dream. Slowly, I saved money for my dream camera setup, and a few months ago, I finally managed to buy it.
My dream is to become an underwater wildlife cinematographer—to work, travel, inspire people, and spread awareness about the beauty and importance of our oceans. I want to film in extreme environments alongside experienced professionals around the world.
One of my biggest dreams is to document the underwater life of the entire Maldives—from the far north to the deep south—and publish it on my own YouTube channel.
It is also my dream to one day participate in major documentary productions such as Blue Planet, Ocean with David Attenborough, or Our Oceans.
Another important goal for me is to raise awareness among Maldivians, encouraging people to stop damaging our fragile marine environment through human activities. Through my lens, I hope to inspire people to protect and respect the underwater world that surrounds us

I’ve been at this for six years now. I’m primarily an underwater photographer who also wants to add video skills to what I can offer. I’m trying to find more opportunities in the conservation and environmental photography and videography world, but it’s been very hard.

I’m also trying to transition into a different career path. I’ve been a tree planter for almost 11 years and want to find a more stable job in science communication while offering photography and videography. I don’t think I’ll ever be able to go fully freelance, as I still need a somewhat stable income, but I do want to keep learning and growing.

I’m a hands-on learner and have been looking for online classes that will teach me what settings to use and how to post-edit properly. I’ve been experimenting on my own, but I have questions as I go and find it challenging when I don’t have anyone to ask.

I want to work with local organizations on and around Vancouver Island, documenting important stories both above and below the water. I’m hoping that through this course, I’ll also gain valuable networking connections that could lead to hands-on opportunities.

column48:
I am highly motivated to deepen my skills in underwater filmmaking because it combines two of my greatest passions: storytelling and the ocean. Being underwater has always felt natural and inspiring to me, and capturing those moments visually adds an entirely new dimension to the experience. I want to continue learning how to document marine life and environmental challenges in a way that is both visually compelling and emotionally powerful.

Underwater filmmaking requires technical precision, patience, and a deep understanding of the environment, and I am eager to further develop these skills. The more I learn, the better I can contribute to raising awareness about ocean conservation and give marine ecosystems a stronger visual voice.
column49:
I am motivated to join Behind the Mask because I want to further develop my skills in underwater filmmaking and combine my experience as a documentary filmmaker with my long-standing passion for diving. Being underwater has shaped my perspective for many years, and I want to refine my technical and storytelling abilities to capture marine environments in a more powerful and professional way.

I am especially interested in deepening my understanding of underwater cinematography, lighting, and narrative structure in order to create stronger visual stories that raise awareness for ocean conservation. Learning from experienced professionals and being part of a community that shares the same passion for the ocean would be a valuable step in my creative and professional growth.

column52:
A little personal info; 
My name is Sandor Vennink. I'm from the Netherlands, 46 years old, 4 kids with my partner Marleen, living in Dordrecht, NL. Diving since I was a kid and currently a certified divemaster. Love photography and diving. I have a successful career in IT, but the passion is gone. 
I'm looking to do something I love doing every day, now that the kids are getting bigger. I love making underwater video's on hollidays. Last year I did an internship as a Divemaster on Bonaire, as I love diving, teaching and sharing knowledge. However, the diving industry (PADI system) is not for me in that sense. Maybe underwater video and photo is more my thing. That is what I want to seriously explore and see where it takes me.
Above water I shoot with a Canon R6, some L glass and a DJI Air 3 drone. I shoot landscapes for fun, events as a freelancer. Underwater I shoot with a Sony ZV-E1 (although most of my video's are made with a GoPro), only for fun/hobby at the moment. I'm a techy guy, lots of IT and engineering knowledge, I'm also a relaxed person with a good sense of humor and immense passion for the sea.
Looking forward to hearing from you.
Kind regards,
Sandor

I'd love to feel confident in gear setup, handling and filming techniques. Actually, I'm looking to build a whole proper workflow from A to Z. Coming from UW-Photography (self-studied), videography really is fascinating but somewhat chaotic at times :D. 

My main goal is to improve my skills while Freediving and Snorkeling with ambient light, so I can create calm, honest underwater short films for social media and storytelling. 
This would directly feed into my passion project, mobula. (www.mobulaocean.com)
It's a ocean-travel-brand in its early stage. Me aside local experts guiding small groups to experience the ocean and its inhabitants in a respectful, connecting way by creating space for real encounters. Different skill levels are welcome and it's mainly on Freediving and Snorkeling.

column48:
My ultimate vision is to use underwater filmmaking as a powerful storytelling tool to connect people emotionally with the ocean. Coming from a freediving background, I’m especially drawn to capturing intimate, calm and human-centered underwater moments — where movement, breath, and light play a central role.

In the long term, I aim to create meaningful visual stories that combine freediving, travel and conservation, whether through documentaries, brand collaborations, or artistic projects. I want my work to inspire respect for the ocean and a deeper awareness of our relationship with it, while continuing to grow both creatively and technically as an underwater filmmaker.
column49:
I have always been inspired by the ocean and by the way underwater images can communicate emotions that words often can’t. As a freediving instructor, the underwater world is already a big part of my life, and filmmaking or even modeling feels like a natural next step to express and share those experiences.
I chose BTM Academy because of its strong focus on storytelling, professionalism, and real-world underwater filmmaking skills. The academy’s approach, mentors, and values resonate deeply with my own goals — not just learning how to film underwater, but how to tell meaningful stories responsibly and creatively.
column51:
I actually applied last year and was selected for the trip, but unfortunately I wasn’t able to attend because I received the confirmation at the same time I had already committed to a professional assignment. It was genuinely disappointing for me not to be able to join, especially knowing the incredible group of mentors involved, many of whom I truly admire.

As I’m currently prioritizing my underwater photography and filmmaking work, my main concern is around scheduling. I’d love to know whether there will be more than one course date available, and if dates are announced with enough advance notice, as I already have some work commitments confirmed throughout the year. I would truly love the opportunity to be able to take part this year if scheduling allows.
column52: I’m very motivated to continue developing my skills in underwater filmmaking and photography, and I’m fully committed to approaching this training with professionalism, curiosity, and respect for the ocean.

column48:
My ultimate goal would be to step away from PADI and SSI and dedicate myself entirely to filming underwater. Documenting the magical underwater world and inspiring people to protect it. Filming pelagic creatures would be the dream (probably some kind of complex, since I'm pretty short). Working for BBC or NatGeo, may be dreaming to big, but that's the goal. Turning my hobby into a career. I have LOTS to learn when it comes to filming though.
My girlfriend, Bella is also very much an underwater lover. A diver, filming enthusiast, who has currently applied for a masters in Science Communication in one of the best universities in the world. Our goal would be to one day work together, sharing our passion and educating people about conservation. We're very serious about turning this into our careers.
column49:
I  have been thinking about investing in proper training to learn underwater filming, for a while now. The moment never seemed right and the funds were low. Last year I decided to take a step back from the diving industry, to really think about what I would love to do with my life. The conclusion was always the same. I grew up watching wildlife documentaries and have always been fascinated by them. A few years after I started scuba diving, I randomly found an action camera during a dive and since then, every time I go fun diving, I take my camera with me. I just love trying to get close to marine life and capture their soul (without disturbing them too much of course). 
About 4 years ago, I heard a talk from Nuno Sá (Portuguese underwater videographer), that really inspired me and made me realise that with some effort and luck I could potentially turn this hobby into a dream job. Since then I've been practicing and I think now is the right time for me to seriously invest some time and money into this. Why BTM Academy? I'd been trying to find underwater filming courses for a few weeks but somehow I hadn't realised BTM had their own courses. Today my girlfriend (who also applied) sent me a link for this page. I have been watching your videos for years now. You guys are sensational. "This is it". I'm ready to make my dream reality.

column48: I was touched, inspired and awe-struck when I first saw the documentaries of "our blue planet II". The visual appealing shots and the storytelling triggered my emotions - be it from seeing big whales to the smallest of creatures. It opened my eyes for a world that some people do not have the privilege to experience. On the other hand it showed me what is worth protecting and what is already lost due to our human hybris. With my underwater filming journey, I would love to trigger these emotions in others so that they understand that life beyond the surface is a miraculous and precious one and that we have to protect it at all costs to preserve the beauty and simply our lives as well.
column49: I already gained some experience in filmmaking and photography "on land". Before and during my studies, I did videography for my own films, travel films and some small client projects. This got a bit lost over the years due to my job and other hobbies. But since I discovered life underwater and put my camera in a waterproof housing, I fell in love with filmmaking again and want to improve my skills under water as well as in general. BTM Academy has such a nice approach to that and the visuals I saw on your website convinced me that I want to become a voice for the ocean to those who do not have the ability or emotional connection to it.

column48:
My journey in underwater filming began with a deep passion for the ocean and a genuine desire to document its beauty and the behavior of its marine life in a professional and responsible way.
 I aspire to further develop my technical and storytelling skills to produce high-quality visual content that combines artistic beauty with educational value with a particular focus on environmental stories and ocean conservation
 My ultimate goal is to work on impactful documentary and commercial projects and to build a sustainable professional career in underwater filming at both regional and international levels.

Its strong reputation project-based approach and direct mentorship make it the right environment to develop my skills and turn my passion for underwater filming into a clear professional path

column52:
Underwater filming has been my true passion for many years.

My long-term goal has always been to develop my skills to the highest possible level not as a casual hobby but as a lifelong professional path. 

This passion led me to make a difficult but deliberate decision to leave my previous job and fully dedicate myself to underwater filmmaking believing that serious creative work requires full commitment and focus.

I live with ulcerative colitis however it does not prevent me from pursuing my goals or practicing diving professionally. 

I have proven this through extensive field experience having dived across the entire Red Sea on both the eastern and western coasts from the far north to the far south.

This experience has given me a deep hands-on understanding of the Red Sea’s marine environment. 

I do prefer diving in temperate waters as cold water can increase the severity of my condition and this is a factor I responsibly consider when planning long-term projects.

I have previously taken a training course with Luca who knows me personally and is familiar with my work my commitment and my long standing passion for underwater filmmaking. 

This experience further strengthened my motivation to pursue structured high-level professional development.

Despite my experience opportunities for advanced professional development in my country remain limited particularly in specialized underwater filmmaking and documentary storytelling.

My ultimate vision is to produce a high-quality documentary film that documents the eastern coastline of the Red Sea, highlighting its ecological value marine biodiversity and global significance.

I believe the timing is ideal especially with the strong governmental support for tourism and the growing international focus on the Red Sea as a major destination and strategic project.

My goal is to contribute to this transformation through authentic impactful and professional visual storytelling that reflects the importance of this moment
